copilot-cli.meta.title: Copilot CLI - DevAI Day Workshop
copilot-cli.meta.description: Learn to use GitHub Copilot CLI to get AI-powered shell command suggestions and explanations directly in your terminal.
copilot-cli.progress.label: Progress
copilot-cli.step1.title: Introduction to Copilot CLI
copilot-cli.step1.p1: GitHub Copilot CLI brings AI-powered assistance directly to your terminal. Instead of switching to a browser to look up a command or its flags, you can ask Copilot in plain language — right where you are working.
copilot-cli.step1.p2: Copilot CLI is a GitHub CLI extension that lets you interact with GitHub Copilot from your terminal. It provides two primary sub-commands:
copilot-cli.step1.li1: gh copilot suggest — Translate a natural-language task description into a shell command
copilot-cli.step1.li2: gh copilot explain — Get a plain-language explanation of any shell command
copilot-cli.step1.p3: Developers spend a significant portion of their time in the terminal. Copilot CLI integrates AI assistance directly into that workflow so you can:
copilot-cli.step1.li3: Avoid context-switching to search engines or documentation sites
copilot-cli.step1.li4: Quickly recall syntax for commands you use infrequently
copilot-cli.step1.li5: Understand unfamiliar commands before running them
copilot-cli.step1.li6: Discover more efficient ways to accomplish common tasks
copilot-cli.step1.li7: Learn new tools and options without leaving the terminal
copilot-cli.step1.p4: By the end of this workshop activity you will be able to:
copilot-cli.step1.li8: Install and authenticate the GitHub Copilot CLI extension
copilot-cli.step1.li9: Use gh copilot suggest to get shell command suggestions from natural language
copilot-cli.step1.li10: Use gh copilot explain to understand unfamiliar commands
copilot-cli.step1.li11: Set up shell aliases (ghcs and ghce) for faster access
copilot-cli.step1.li12: Integrate Copilot CLI into everyday terminal workflows
copilot-cli.step1.strong1: Before you begin, make sure you have:
copilot-cli.step1.li13: GitHub CLI (gh) v2.x or later installed and authenticated — Install from cli.github.com
copilot-cli.step1.li14: A GitHub Copilot license — Individual, Business, or Enterprise. Learn more about plans
copilot-cli.step1.li15: A supported shell — Bash, Zsh, Fish, or PowerShell
copilot-cli.step1.p5: Verify that gh is installed and authenticated by running:
copilot-cli.step1.p6: You should see a confirmation that you are logged in to github.com.
copilot-cli.step2.title: Exercise 1: Install Copilot CLI
copilot-cli.step2.p1: Copilot CLI is distributed as a GitHub CLI extension. Extensions are installed with a single command and kept up to date with gh extension upgrade.
copilot-cli.step2.p2: Run the following command to install the GitHub Copilot CLI extension:
copilot-cli.step2.p3: Confirm the extension is installed and check which version is active:
copilot-cli.step2.p4: You should see output similar to:
copilot-cli.step2.p5: Explore what Copilot CLI can do:
copilot-cli.step2.p6: Update the extension at any time with:
copilot-cli.step2.p7: On first use, Copilot CLI may prompt you to grant additional permissions. Follow the on-screen instructions to complete the OAuth flow in your browser. After authentication, return to your terminal — you are ready to start using Copilot CLI.
copilot-cli.step3.title: Exercise 2: Suggest Commands
copilot-cli.step3.p1: gh copilot suggest converts a plain-language description into a shell command. You describe what you want to accomplish, and Copilot returns a command you can copy, run, or refine.
copilot-cli.step3.p2: You can tell Copilot which shell environment you are targeting with the --target flag:
copilot-cli.step3.p3: Supported targets are shell, git, and gh. When omitted, Copilot infers the best target from your description.
copilot-cli.step3.p4: After Copilot suggests a command, it presents an interactive menu:
copilot-cli.step3.li1: Copy command to clipboard — Paste it into your terminal manually
copilot-cli.step3.li2: Explain command — Get a plain-language explanation before running it
copilot-cli.step3.li3: Execute command — Run the command immediately in your current shell
copilot-cli.step3.li4: Revise command — Provide additional context to refine the suggestion
copilot-cli.step3.li5: Ask Copilot to suggest a command to list all files modified in the last 7 days:
copilot-cli.step3.li6: Ask for a command to find all files larger than 100 MB in your home directory:
copilot-cli.step3.li7: Use the --target git flag to ask for a Git command:
copilot-cli.step3.li8: Try the interactive Revise command option to add more specificity to one of the suggestions
copilot-cli.step3.p5: The more specific your description, the more accurate the suggestion. Include details like file types, time ranges, or output formats to get better results.
copilot-cli.step4.title: Exercise 3: Explain Commands
copilot-cli.step4.p1: gh copilot explain takes any shell command and returns a clear, plain-language explanation of what it does — including each flag and argument. This is invaluable when you encounter unfamiliar commands in scripts, documentation, or Stack Overflow answers.
copilot-cli.step4.li4: Paste a command from a script you are unfamiliar with and ask Copilot to explain it
copilot-cli.step4.p2: Always use gh copilot explain before running an unfamiliar command with elevated privileges (sudo) or destructive flags (-rf, --force, --delete). Understanding a command before running it is a healthy habit.
copilot-cli.step4.p3: You can pipe command output directly to Copilot explain for contextual help:
copilot-cli.step4.p4: This is useful for understanding error messages or log output without copying and pasting manually.
copilot-cli.step5.title: Exercise 4: Shell Alias Setup
copilot-cli.step5.p1: Typing gh copilot suggest or gh copilot explain every time is verbose. Setting up short shell aliases — ghcs (GitHub Copilot Suggest) and ghce (GitHub Copilot Explain) — makes Copilot CLI feel like a natural part of your workflow.
copilot-cli.step5.p2: Copilot CLI can generate the alias configuration for your shell automatically:
copilot-cli.step5.p3: Replace bash with your shell (zsh, fish, or powershell). The command prints shell-specific configuration to standard output.
copilot-cli.step5.p4: Add the aliases to your shell configuration file:
copilot-cli.step5.p6: After editing the file, reload your shell configuration:
copilot-cli.step5.p7: For Fish, add the configuration to your Fish config file:
copilot-cli.step5.p8: To make it permanent, add the following line to ~/.config/fish/config.fish:
copilot-cli.step5.p9: For PowerShell on Windows, add the following to your PowerShell profile ($PROFILE):
copilot-cli.step5.p10: Open a new terminal session (or reload your shell config) and test the aliases:
copilot-cli.step5.p11: ghcs is a shorthand for gh copilot suggest and ghce is a shorthand for gh copilot explain. They accept exactly the same arguments as the full commands.
copilot-cli.step6.title: Challenge: Real-World Tasks
copilot-cli.step6.p1: Now it is time to put everything together. Complete all three tasks below using only Copilot CLI suggestions — no manual searches or documentation. For each task, use ghcs (or gh copilot suggest) to generate the command, then use ghce (or gh copilot explain) to verify you understand what it does before running it.
copilot-cli.step6.p2: Use Copilot CLI to find and remove all .DS_Store files from a Git repository (without deleting the files from disk, only from Git tracking).
copilot-cli.step6.li1: Hint: You will need a git command and a follow-up shell command
copilot-cli.step6.li2: Use ghcs --target git for the Git-specific part
copilot-cli.step6.p3: Your application generates log files in /var/log/app/. Use Copilot CLI to:
copilot-cli.step6.li3: Count the number of ERROR lines across all .log files in that directory
copilot-cli.step6.li4: Extract the 10 most recent unique error messages, sorted by frequency
copilot-cli.step6.li6: Use ghce to understand the pipeline before running it
copilot-cli.step6.p4: Use Copilot CLI to generate commands that:
copilot-cli.step6.li7: List all Docker containers that exited more than 24 hours ago
copilot-cli.step6.li8: Remove all stopped containers, unused networks, dangling images, and build cache in a single command
copilot-cli.step6.li9: Always use ghce to review destructive commands before executing them
copilot-cli.step6.p5: After completing the three tasks, consider:
copilot-cli.step6.li10: How did using Copilot CLI compare to your usual workflow for looking up commands?
copilot-cli.step6.li11: Which task benefited most from the --target flag?
copilot-cli.step6.li12: Did any suggestion require refinement? What additional context helped?
copilot-cli.step7.title: Tips & Best Practices
copilot-cli.step7.p1: Make the most of GitHub Copilot CLI with these tips and best practices gathered from real-world use.
copilot-cli.step7.li1: Be specific — Include file types, time ranges, and output format in your description. "list log files" is less effective than "list all .log files in /var/log modified in the last 24 hours, sorted by size"
copilot-cli.step7.li2: Mention the tool or ecosystem — Saying "a git command to …" or "a Docker command to …" helps Copilot choose the right tool
copilot-cli.step7.li3: Use the --target flag — Setting --target git or --target gh scopes suggestions and makes them more accurate
copilot-cli.step7.li4: Iterate with "Revise" — The first suggestion may not be exactly what you need. Use the interactive Revise option to add constraints or change the approach
copilot-cli.step7.li5: Always explain before running — Particularly for destructive commands (rm -rf, docker system prune, database drops). Use ghce to review what a command does before executing it
copilot-cli.step7.li6: Prefer dry-run flags — Many tools support --dry-run or -n flags. Ask Copilot to include them: "list the files that would be deleted, without actually deleting them"
copilot-cli.step7.li7: Review multi-piped commands carefully — Long pipelines can have unintended side effects. Break them into stages if you are unsure
copilot-cli.step7.li8: Pair with your shell history — After Copilot suggests a command and you run it successfully, it is added to your shell history for easy recall with the up arrow
copilot-cli.step7.li9: Use it for learning — Ask Copilot to suggest a command you already know, then use explain to see if there is a more efficient variant you were not aware of
copilot-cli.step7.li10: Combine with scripts — Use Copilot CLI interactively to prototype shell scripts, then copy the final commands into a .sh file
copilot-cli.step7.li11: Use the aliases — Once you have set up ghcs and ghce, using Copilot CLI becomes as natural as running any other command

copilot-cli.step7.li18: Use gh copilot explain to understand unfamiliar commands
copilot-cli.step7.li19: Use short aliases (ghcs / ghce) for faster access
copilot-cli.step7.li20: Apply Copilot CLI to real-world terminal tasks safely and effectively
